channel.title: Const on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Const on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2540 — Cannot Assign to Read-Only Property
item.description:
TS2540 — Cannot assign to ‘X’ because it is a read-only property

TS2540 occurs when you try to modify a value that’s marked as readonly, const, or comes from a Readonly<T> type. TypeScript prevents mutation of these values at compile time.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Assigning to a const variable
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2540: Cannot assign to 'x' because it is a constant

// Cause 2: Modifying a readonly property
interface Config {
 readonly host: string;
}
const config: Config = { host: "localhost" };
config.host = "other.com"; // TS2540

// Cause 3: Modifying a readonly array
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // TS2540

// Cause 4: Modifying a property in Readonly<T>
interface User {
 name: string;
}
const user: Readonly<User> = { name: "Alice" };
user.name = "Bob"; // TS2540

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’let’ instead of ‘const’ for mutable variables

// Wrong
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2540

// Correct
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK

Fix 2: Remove ‘readonly’ from the property

// Wrong
interface Config {
 readonly host: string;
}

// Correct — if mutation is intended
interface Config {
 host: string;
}

Fix 3: Create a mutable copy

// Wrong
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // TS2540

// Correct
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
const mutableArr = [...arr]; // Spread into mutable array
mutableArr.push(4);

Fix 4: Use a type assertion to bypass readonly (use carefully)

// Use only when you're certain mutation is safe
const config: Readonly<Config> = { host: "localhost" };
(config as Config).host = "other.com"; // Bypasses readonly

Examples

// Example 1: Readonly class property
class ApiClient {
 readonly baseUrl = "https://api.example.com";
}
const client = new ApiClient();
client.baseUrl = "https://other.com"; // TS2540

// Example 2: Object.freeze returns Readonly
const frozen = Object.freeze({ x: 1, y: 2 });
frozen.x = 10; // TS2540

// Example 3: Tuple assignment
const tuple: readonly [string, number] = ["hello", 42];
tuple[0] = "world"; // TS2540

Related Errors

  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — readonly prevents reading in some contexts.
  • TS2588: Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable — specific to const variables.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2588 — Cannot Assign to 'const' Variable
item.description:
TS2588 — Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable

TS2588 occurs when you try to reassign a variable declared with const. Unlike let, const variables cannot be reassigned after initialization. This is a fundamental JavaScript/TypeScript rule enforced at compile time.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Reassigning a const variable
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2588: Cannot assign to 'x' because it is a constant

// Cause 2: Trying to reassign in a loop
const counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++; // TS2588
}

// Cause 3: Reassigning a function parameter (const in destructuring)
function process({ id, name }: { id: number; name: string }) {
 id = 0; // TS2588: 'id' is a const
}

// Cause 4: Increment/decrement operator on const
const count = 0;
count++; // TS2588

Solutions

Fix 1: Use ’let’ for variables that need reassignment

// Wrong
const x = 10;
x = 20; // TS2588

// Correct
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK

Fix 2: Use ’let’ for counters

// Wrong
const counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++; // TS2588
}

// Correct
let counter = 0;
for (let i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 counter++;
}

Fix 3: Use a new variable instead of reassignment

// Wrong
const count = 0;
count++;

// Correct — create a new value
const count = 0;
const newCount = count + 1;

Fix 4: Use mutable objects with const

// Const prevents reassignment, not mutation
const arr = [1, 2, 3];
arr.push(4); // OK — mutation is allowed
arr = [5, 6, 7]; // TS2588 — reassignment is not

// If you need to "replace" the array
const arr2 = [...arr, 4]; // New array, same reference

Examples

// Example 1: React state with const
function Counter() {
 const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
 // count++; // TS2588 — can't reassign const
 setCount(count + 1); // Correct — use setter
}

// Example 2: Trying to swap values
let a = 1;
let b = 2;
const temp = a;
a = b;
b = temp; // OK — temp is const but we're not reassigning it
// But: temp = 5; // TS2588

// Example 3: Destructured const
const [first, second] = [1, 2];
// first = 10; // TS2588

Related Errors

  • TS2540: Cannot assign to read-only property — readonly object properties.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — readonly access issues.
  • TS2322: Type is not assignable — broader type assignment issues.

item.title: [Solution] JavaScript var Deprecated — Use let/const for Block Scope
item.description:
[Solution] JavaScript var Deprecated — Use let/const for Block Scope

While var is not technically removed from JavaScript, it is widely considered deprecated in modern codebases. The let and const declarations (introduced in ES6/2015) provide block scoping, prevent hoisting-related bugs, and make code behavior more predictable. Major linters (ESLint no-var rule), style guides, and TypeScript all recommend replacing var with let or const.

Why var is Problematic

// Problem 1: Function scope instead of block scope
for (var i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
 setTimeout(() => console.log(i), 100);
}
// Prints: 3, 3, 3 (not 0, 1, 2 — var is function-scoped)

// Problem 2: Hoisting leads to unexpected undefined
console.log(x); // undefined (not a ReferenceError!)
var x = 5;

// Problem 3: Re-declaration is allowed (silent bug)
var count = 1;
var count = 2; // no error — easy to make mistakes

// Problem 4: Global pollution
function example() {
 var global = "oops"; // leaks to window.global
}
console.log(window.global); // "oops"

Old Code (var)

// var in loops — classic closure bug
var buttons = document.querySelectorAll("button");
for (var i = 0; i < buttons.length; i++) {
 buttons[i].addEventListener("click", function() {
 console.log("Button " + i); // always prints the last value of i
 });
}

// var hoisting
console.log(name); // undefined (no error!)
var name = "Alice";

// var re-declaration
var x = 1;
var x = 2; // silent — no error

// var in if blocks — leaks out
if (true) {
 var temp = 42;
}
console.log(temp); // 42 — leaked out of the if block

New Code (let/const)

// let in loops — correct closure behavior
const buttons = document.querySelectorAll("button");
for (let i = 0; i < buttons.length; i++) {
 buttons[i].addEventListener("click", function() {
 console.log("Button " + i); // prints correct index
 });
}

// let — no hoisting surprise
// console.log(name); // ReferenceError: Cannot access 'name' before initialization
let name = "Alice";

// const — cannot re-declare or reassign
const x = 1;
// x = 2; // TypeError: Assignment to constant variable
// const x = 3; // SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared

// let in if blocks — properly scoped
if (true) {
 let temp = 42;
}
// console.log(temp); // ReferenceError: temp is not defined

Rules of Thumb

// Rule 1: Use const by default — the value never changes
const API_URL = "https://api.example.com";
const config = { timeout: 5000 };
const users = ["Alice", "Bob"];

// Rule 2: Use let when the value needs to change
let counter = 0;
let currentUser = null;

// Rule 3: Never use var in new code
// var x = 5; // DON'T DO THIS

// Rule 4: Destructuring with const/let
const { name, age } = user;
let [first, ...rest] = items;

// Rule 5: for...of with const for loop body
const items = [1, 2, 3];
for (const item of items) {
 console.log(item); // each iteration gets a new binding
}

// Rule 6: for with index uses let
for (let i = 0; i < items.length; i++) {
 console.log(i, items[i]);
}

var vs let vs const Comparison

Feature var let const
Scope Function Block Block
Hoisting Yes (initialized as undefined) Yes (TDZ) Yes (TDZ)
Re-declaration Allowed Not allowed Not allowed
Reassignment Allowed Allowed Not allowed
Global object property Yes (window.x) No No
Temporal Dead Zone No Yes Yes

TDZ = Temporal Dead Zone — accessing the variable before its declaration throws a ReferenceError.
